---
title: "Sequence Assembler"
description: "Contiguous temporal sequence segmentation for sequence models"
---

## Overview

The `SequenceAssembler` (`pipelines/SequenceAssembler.hpp`) segments a flat
feature matrix and associated time indices into contiguous temporal sequences.
This is essential for sequence models like HMMs that require observations
grouped into runs of consecutive time steps.

TensorData may have gaps in its `TimeFrameIndex` rows (e.g., frames 100–200
then 500–600). The SequenceAssembler detects these gaps and splits the flat
matrix into separate `SequenceSegment` objects, one per contiguous run.

## Gap Detection

A **gap** occurs when consecutive TimeFrameIndex values differ by more than 1:

```
times[i+1] - times[i] > 1  →  new sequence starts at i+1
```

For example, given times `{0, 1, 2, 10, 11, 12}`:

- Run 1: indices 0–2 (times 0, 1, 2)
- Run 2: indices 3–5 (times 10, 11, 12)

## Data Structures

### SequenceSegment

```cpp
struct SequenceSegment {
    arma::mat features;               // features × segment_length (mlpack layout)
    arma::Row<std::size_t> labels;    // 1 × segment_length (empty if unlabeled)
    std::vector<TimeFrameIndex> times; // Original time index per column
};
```

### SequenceAssemblerConfig

```cpp
struct SequenceAssemblerConfig {
    std::size_t min_sequence_length = 2;  // Discard shorter sequences
};
```

The default `min_sequence_length = 2` filters out single-frame sequences, which
carry no temporal transition information for HMMs.

## API

Two overloads are provided — one with labels (for training) and one without
(for prediction):

```cpp
// Labeled segmentation (training)
std::vector<SequenceSegment> SequenceAssembler::segment(
    arma::mat const & features,
    arma::Row<std::size_t> const & labels,
    std::vector<TimeFrameIndex> const & times,
    SequenceAssemblerConfig const & config = {});

// Unlabeled segmentation (prediction)
std::vector<SequenceSegment> SequenceAssembler::segment(
    arma::mat const & features,
    std::vector<TimeFrameIndex> const & times,
    SequenceAssemblerConfig const & config = {});
```

### Preconditions

- `features.n_cols == times.size()`
- `labels.n_elem == times.size()` (labeled overload)
- `times` must be sorted in ascending order

## Usage Example

```cpp
// After FeatureConverter produces a flat matrix:
auto converted = convertTensorToArma(tensor, config);

// Build TimeFrameIndex vector from valid row indices
std::vector<TimeFrameIndex> times;
for (auto idx : converted.valid_row_indices) {
    times.emplace_back(static_cast<int64_t>(idx));
}

// Segment for HMM training
auto segments = SequenceAssembler::segment(
    converted.matrix, labels, times,
    {.min_sequence_length = 2});

// Each segment is a contiguous temporal run
for (auto const & seg : segments) {
    // seg.features: features × segment_length
    // seg.labels:   1 × segment_length
    // seg.times:    original time indices
}
```

## Pipeline Integration

The SequenceAssembler slots into the ClassificationPipeline between label
assembly and training when the selected model supports sequence-based training.
The pipeline flow becomes:

```
ValidatingFeatures → ConvertingFeatures → AssemblingLabels →
SegmentingSequences → Training → Predicting → ComputingMetrics → WritingOutput
```

Models that implement `trainSequences()` / `predictSequences()` (e.g., HMM)
receive the segmented sequences directly. Frame-based models skip the
segmentation stage entirely.

## Test Coverage

18 test cases covering:

- Empty input
- Single-frame sequences (filtered/kept by config)
- No gaps (single contiguous run)
- All gaps (every frame isolated)
- Multiple segments with varying gap sizes
- `min_sequence_length` filtering at various thresholds
- Unlabeled segmentation (empty labels)
- Multi-row feature preservation
- Boundary condition: gap of exactly 2
- Large contiguous blocks (1000 frames)
- Negative TimeFrameIndex values
- Two-frame sequences (minimum viable HMM input)
